startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 24-year-old woman presents to the cardiology outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of exertional dyspnoea, palpitations, and occasional pre-syncope. She has no prior cardiac history. Vitals: HR 52/min regular, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98%. You are the cardiology registrar evaluating her.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified complete heart block with impaired cardiac output, applied the Frank-Starling law and cardiac output equation, and chose appropriate management with permanent pacemaker implantation. Your understanding of cardiac automaticity, the conduction system, and haemodynamic principles was exemplary.

You identified some features but missed key physiological connections. Review the cardiac conduction system, the SA node as primary pacemaker, the Frank-Starling law, the Fick principle, and baroreceptor-mediated blood pressure regulation.
Review cardiovascular physiology: SA node automaticity, cardiac output (SV x HR), the Frank-Starling law, heart sounds, baroreceptor reflexes, gap junctions, and the Fick principle.

params.text:
Clinical Assessment

On cardiovascular examination:

  • Pulse: 52/min, regular, normal volume. Jugular venous pressure shows intermittent cannon A waves.
  • Auscultation: S1 varies in intensity. S2 is normally split. Occasional S4 is heard. No murmurs.
  • BP: 100/65 mmHg with a wide pulse pressure

ECG findings: Complete AV dissociation — atrial rate 80/min, ventricular rate 48/min. P waves and QRS complexes are completely independent. Narrow QRS complexes suggest a junctional escape rhythm.

Physiological context: The SA node fires at an intrinsic rate of 70-80 bpm and is the primary pacemaker due to its property of automaticity — the ability to generate action potentials spontaneously without neural input. Gap junctions between cardiac myocytes allow the heart to function as a functional syncytium, enabling coordinated contraction. The long refractory period (~250 ms) prevents tetanic contraction.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's heart rate is 52/min. Cardiac output = Stroke Volume x Heart Rate. If her stroke volume is 70 mL, what is her cardiac output, and how does this compare to normal?

alternatives.text: 3.64 L/min — significantly below the normal resting cardiac output of approximately 5 L/min, explaining her symptoms

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Cardiac output = 70 mL x 52/min = 3,640 mL/min = 3.64 L/min. Normal resting cardiac output is approximately 5 L/min. Her reduced cardiac output (27% below normal) explains the exertional dyspnoea, reduced exercise tolerance, and pre-syncope. By the Frank-Starling law, her stroke volume may increase somewhat with increased preload, but the bradycardia limits overall cardiac output.

alternatives.text: 5.2 L/min — within normal range, so her symptoms must have another cause

feedback.title: Incorrect calculation
feedback.subtitle: 70 mL x 52/min = 3,640 mL/min (3.64 L/min), not 5.2 L/min. Normal resting cardiac output is approximately 5 L/min. Her cardiac output is significantly reduced, which explains her symptoms of exertional dyspnoea and pre-syncope.

alternatives.text: 3.64 L/min — but this is normal for a young woman

feedback.title: Calculation is correct, interpretation is wrong
feedback.subtitle: While 3.64 L/min is indeed the correct calculation (70 x 52), normal resting cardiac output is approximately 5 L/min regardless of sex. A cardiac output of 3.64 L/min represents a 27% reduction from normal, which explains her symptoms of exertional dyspnoea and pre-syncope.

params.text:
Haemodynamic Assessment

Echocardiography: Normal LV size and systolic function (EF 60%). No structural heart disease. No valvular abnormalities.

Exercise stress test: Chronotropic incompetence — heart rate fails to rise above 60/min despite maximal exertion. Symptoms of dyspnoea and light-headedness reproduced at peak exercise.

Physiological correlation:

  • Frank-Starling law: The force of contraction increases with increasing venous return (preload). During exercise, venous return increases, but in this patient, the heart rate cannot increase to augment cardiac output — this is the limiting factor.
  • Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch sense the low blood pressure and attempt short-term compensation by increasing sympathetic tone, but this cannot overcome the conduction block.
  • First heart sound (S1) varies in intensity because the AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) close at varying positions depending on the PR interval — which varies randomly in complete heart block.
  • Fick principle: Cardiac output = O2 consumption / arteriovenous O2 difference. Her body compensates for low CO by increasing O2 extraction (widening A-V O2 difference).

branchingQuestion.question: Given complete heart block with symptomatic bradycardia and chronotropic incompetence in a young patient, what is the definitive management?

alternatives.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker (DDD) to restore AV synchrony and allow rate-responsive heart rate increase

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Complete heart block with symptoms is a Class I indication for permanent pacing. A DDD (dual-chamber, dual-sensing, dual-response) pacemaker restores AV synchrony — pacing both atria and ventricles — which optimises cardiac output by the Frank-Starling mechanism. Rate-responsive mode allows heart rate to increase with activity, correcting chronotropic incompetence.

alternatives.text: Long-term oral atropine to increase heart rate via vagal blockade

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Oral atropine is not effective for long-term management of complete heart block. Atropine works by blocking vagal input to the SA node, but in complete heart block, the problem is at the AV node/His-Purkinje level. Permanent pacing is the definitive treatment for symptomatic complete heart block.

alternatives.text: Isoproterenol infusion to increase heart rate, followed by observation

feedback.title: Temporary measure only
feedback.subtitle: Isoproterenol (a beta-agonist) can temporarily increase heart rate and is used as a bridge in acute situations, but it is not a long-term solution. Complete heart block requires permanent pacing. Continuous isoproterenol carries risks of arrhythmias and is not feasible for chronic management.

params.text:
Outcome and Key Learning Points

The patient undergoes successful implantation of a dual-chamber rate-responsive pacemaker (DDDR). At 6-week follow-up, her heart rate is 72/min at rest with appropriate rate response during exercise. Symptoms of dyspnoea and pre-syncope have completely resolved. Calculated cardiac output is now approximately 5 L/min.

Key cardiovascular physiology points:

  • The SA node fires at 70-80 bpm — the primary pacemaker with automaticity
  • The long refractory period (~250 ms) prevents tetanic contraction
  • Cardiac output = stroke volume x heart rate; normal ~5 L/min
  • The Frank-Starling law: force of contraction increases with increased preload (venous return)
  • S1 is produced by closure of AV valves (mitral and tricuspid)
  • Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch mediate short-term BP regulation
  • The Fick principle: CO = O2 consumption / A-V O2 difference
  • Gap junctions allow the heart to function as a functional syncytium
